From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Missionaries](/w/index.php?title=Missionaries&redirect=no "Missionaries"))

Member of a religious group sent into an area to promote their faith

For other uses, see [Missionary (disambiguation)](/wiki/Missionary_(disambiguation) "Missionary (disambiguation)").

"Mission partner" redirects here. For other uses, see [Mission (disambiguation)](/wiki/Mission_(disambiguation) "Mission (disambiguation)") and [Partner (disambiguation)](/wiki/Partner_(disambiguation) "Partner (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/IVE_Missionaries_in_Papua_New_Guinea.jpg/500px-IVE_Missionaries_in_Papua_New_Guinea.jpg)](/wiki/File:IVE_Missionaries_in_Papua_New_Guinea.jpg)

[Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") missionaries in [Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Papua_New_Guinea "Papua New Guinea")

A **missionary** is a member of a [religious group](/wiki/Religious_denomination "Religious denomination") who is sent into an area in order to promote its faith or provide services to people, such as [education](/wiki/Education "Education"), [literacy](/wiki/Literacy "Literacy"), [social justice](/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice"), [health care](/wiki/Health_care "Health care"), and [economic development](/wiki/Economic_development "Economic development").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Thomas_Hale_2003-2)

In the [Latin translation of the Bible](/wiki/Bible_translations_into_Latin "Bible translations into Latin"), [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") says the word when he sends the disciples into areas and commands them to preach the [gospel](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel") in his name. The term is most commonly used in reference to [Christian missions](/wiki/Christian_mission "Christian mission"), but it can also be used in reference to any [creed](/wiki/Creed "Creed") or [ideology](/wiki/Ideology "Ideology").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The word *mission* originated in 1598 when [Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits"), the members of the [Society of Jesus](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus") sent members abroad, derived from the Latin *missionem* ([nom.](/wiki/Nominative_case "Nominative case") *missio*), meaning 'act of sending' or *mittere*, meaning 'to send'.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## By religion

### Buddhist missions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missionary&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Buddhist missions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Buddhism_Growth_in_Hellenic_World.png/250px-Buddhism_Growth_in_Hellenic_World.png)](/wiki/File:Buddhism_Growth_in_Hellenic_World.png)

Buddhist [proselytism](/wiki/Proselytism "Proselytism") at the time of king Ashoka (260–218 BCE), according to his [Edicts](/wiki/Edicts_of_Ashoka "Edicts of Ashoka")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Central_Asian_Buddhist_Monks.jpeg/250px-Central_Asian_Buddhist_Monks.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Central_Asian_Buddhist_Monks.jpeg)

[Central Asian](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") [Buddhist monk](/wiki/Bhikkhu "Bhikkhu") teaching a [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_people "Chinese people") monk. [Bezeklik](/wiki/Bezeklik "Bezeklik"), 9th–10th century; although [Albert von Le Coq](/wiki/Albert_von_Le_Coq "Albert von Le Coq") (1913) assumed the [blue-eyed](/wiki/Blue_eyes "Blue eyes"), [red-haired](/wiki/Red_hair "Red hair") monk was a [Tocharian](/wiki/Tocharians "Tocharians"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) modern scholarship has identified similar [Caucasian figures](/wiki/Caucasian_race "Caucasian race") of [the same cave temple](/wiki/File:BezeklikSogdianMerchants.jpg "File:BezeklikSogdianMerchants.jpg") (No. 9) as ethnic [Sogdians](/wiki/Sogdia "Sogdia"),[[6]](#cite_note-gasparini_2014_pp134-163-6) an [Eastern Iranian people](/wiki/Eastern_Iranian_people "Eastern Iranian people") who inhabited [Turfan](/wiki/Turfan "Turfan") as an ethnic minority community during the phases of [Tang Chinese](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") (7th–8th century) and [Uyghur rule](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Qocho "Kingdom of Qocho") (9th–13th century).[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The first Buddhist missionaries were called "Dharma Bhanaks", and some[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] see a missionary charge in the symbolism behind the Buddhist wheel, which is said to travel all over the earth bringing Buddhism with it. The Emperor [Ashoka](/wiki/Ashoka "Ashoka") was a significant early Buddhist missioner. In the 3rd century BCE, [Dharmaraksita](/wiki/Dharmaraksita "Dharmaraksita")—among others—was sent out by emperor Ashoka to proselytize[[8]](#cite_note-8) and initially the [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist") tradition through the Indian [Maurya Empire](/wiki/Maurya_Empire "Maurya Empire"), but later into the Mediterranean as far as Greece. Gradually, all India and the neighboring island of [Ceylon](/wiki/Ceylon "Ceylon") were converted. Then, in later periods, Buddhism spread eastward and southeastward to the present lands of [Burma](/wiki/Burma "Burma"), [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos"), [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia"), [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), and [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia").[[9]](#cite_note-Welty,_Paul_Thomas_1966_pg._77-9)

Buddhism was spread among the [Turkic people](/wiki/Turkic_people "Turkic people") during the 2nd and 3rd centuries BCE into modern-day Pakistan, [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir"), [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), eastern and coastal [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan"), [Turkmenistan](/wiki/Turkmenistan "Turkmenistan"), and [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan"). It was also taken into China brought by [Kasyapa Matanga](/wiki/Kasyapa_Matanga "Kasyapa Matanga") in the 2nd century [CE](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era"), [Lokaksema](/wiki/Lokaksema_(Buddhist_monk) "Lokaksema (Buddhist monk)") and [An Shigao](/wiki/An_Shigao "An Shigao") translated Buddhist sutras into Chinese. [Dharmarakṣa](/wiki/Dharmarak%E1%B9%A3a "Dharmarakṣa") was one of the greatest translators of Mahayana Buddhist scriptures into Chinese. Dharmaraksa came to the Chinese capital of [Luoyang](/wiki/Luoyang "Luoyang") in 266 CE, where he made the first known translations of the [Lotus Sutra](/wiki/Lotus_Sutra "Lotus Sutra") and the [Dasabhumika Sutra](/wiki/Dasabhumika_Sutra "Dasabhumika Sutra"), which were to become some of the classic texts of Chinese Mahayana Buddhism. Altogether, Dharmaraksa translated around 154 [Hīnayāna](/wiki/H%C4%ABnay%C4%81na "Hīnayāna") and [Mahāyāna](/wiki/Mah%C4%81y%C4%81na "Mahāyāna") *[sutras](/wiki/Sutra "Sutra")*, representing most of the important texts of Buddhism available in the Western Regions. His [proselytizing](/wiki/Proselytizing "Proselytizing") is said to have converted many to Buddhism in China, and made [Chang'an](/wiki/Chang%27an "Chang'an"), present-day [Xi'an](/wiki/Xi%27an "Xi'an"), a major center of Buddhism. Buddhism expanded rapidly, especially among the common people, and by 381 most of the people of northwest China were Buddhist. Winning converts also among the rulers and scholars, by the end of the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") Buddhism was found everywhere in China.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

[Marananta](/wiki/Marananta "Marananta") brought Buddhism to the Korean Peninsula in the 4th century. [Seong of Baekje](/wiki/Seong_of_Baekje "Seong of Baekje"), known as a great patron of [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") in [Korea](/wiki/Korea "Korea"), built many temples and welcomed priests bringing Buddhist texts directly from India. In 528, Baekje officially adopted Buddhism as its state religion. He sent tribute missions to Liang in 534 and 541, on the second occasion requesting artisans as well as various Buddhist works and a teacher. According to Chinese records, all these requests were granted. A subsequent mission was sent in 549, only to find the Liang capital in the hands of the rebel [Hou Jing](/wiki/Hou_Jing "Hou Jing"), who threw them in prison for lamenting the fall of the capital. He is credited with having sent a mission in 538 to [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") that brought an image of [Shakyamuni](/wiki/Shakyamuni "Shakyamuni") and several sutras to the Japanese court. This has traditionally been considered the official introduction of Buddhism to Japan. An account of this is given in *[Gangōji Garan Engi](/wiki/Gang%C5%8Dji_Garan_Engi "Gangōji Garan Engi")*. First supported by the Soga clan, Buddhism rose over the objections of the pro-Shinto [Mononobe](/wiki/Mononobe "Mononobe"),[[11]](#cite_note-Crim,_Keith_1989_pg._523-11) and Buddhism entrenched itself in Japan with the conversion of Prince [Shotoku Taishi](/wiki/Shotoku_Taishi "Shotoku Taishi").[[9]](#cite_note-Welty,_Paul_Thomas_1966_pg._77-9) When in 710 [Emperor Shomu](/wiki/Emperor_Shomu "Emperor Shomu") established a new capital at [Nara](/wiki/Nara,_Nara "Nara, Nara") with urban [grid plan](/wiki/Grid_plan#Asia_from_the_first_millennium_AD "Grid plan") modeled after the capital of China, Buddhism received official support and began to flourish.[[11]](#cite_note-Crim,_Keith_1989_pg._523-11)

[Padmasambhava](/wiki/Padmasambhava "Padmasambhava"), The Lotus Born, was a sage guru from Oḍḍiyāna who is said to have transmitted Vajrayana Buddhism to [Bhutan](/wiki/Bhutan "Bhutan") and [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet") and neighbouring countries in the 8th century.

The use of missions, councils, and monastic institutions influenced the emergence of Christian missions and organizations, which developed similar structures in places that were formerly Buddhist missions.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

During the 19th and 20th centuries, Western intellectuals such as [Schopenhauer](/wiki/Arthur_Schopenhauer "Arthur Schopenhauer"), [Henry David Thoreau](/wiki/Henry_David_Thoreau "Henry David Thoreau"), [Max Müller](/wiki/Max_M%C3%BCller "Max Müller"), and [esoteric](/wiki/Esoteric "Esoteric") societies such as the [Theosophical Society](/wiki/Theosophical_Society "Theosophical Society") of [H.P. Blavatsky](/wiki/H.P._Blavatsky "H.P. Blavatsky"), [The Buddhist Society of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/Charles_Henry_Allan_Bennett#Buddhist_Society_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "Charles Henry Allan Bennett") and the [Buddhist Society, London](/wiki/Buddhist_Society,_London "Buddhist Society, London") spread interest in Buddhism. Writers such as [Hermann Hesse](/wiki/Hermann_Hesse "Hermann Hesse") and [Jack Kerouac](/wiki/Jack_Kerouac "Jack Kerouac"), in the West, and the [hippie](/wiki/Hippie "Hippie") generation of the late 1960s and early 1970s led to a re-discovery of Buddhism. During the 20th and 21st centuries Buddhism has again been propagated by missionaries into the West such as [Ananda Metteyya](/wiki/Charles_Henry_Allan_Bennett "Charles Henry Allan Bennett") ([Theravada Buddhism](/wiki/Theravada_Buddhism "Theravada Buddhism")),[[13]](#cite_note-13) [Suzuki Daisetsu Teitarō](/wiki/Suzuki_Daisetsu_Teitar%C5%8D "Suzuki Daisetsu Teitarō") ([Zen Buddhism](/wiki/Zen_Buddhism "Zen Buddhism")),[[14]](#cite_note-14) the [Dalai Lama](/wiki/Dalai_Lama "Dalai Lama")[[15]](#cite_note-15) and monks including [Lama Surya Das](/wiki/Lama_Surya_Das "Lama Surya Das") (Tibetan Buddhism). [Tibetan Buddhism](/wiki/Tibetan_Buddhism "Tibetan Buddhism") has been significantly active and successful in the West since the Chinese takeover of Tibet in 1959. Today Buddhists make a decent proportion of several countries in the West such as [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").

In Canada, the immense popularity and goodwill ushered in by [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet")'s [Dalai Lama](/wiki/Dalai_Lama "Dalai Lama") (who has been made honorary Canadian citizen) put Buddhism in a favourable light in the country. Many non-Asian Canadians embraced Buddhism in various traditions and some have become leaders in their respective [sanghas](/wiki/Sangha_(Buddhism) "Sangha (Buddhism)").

In the early 1990s, the French Buddhist Union (UBF, founded in 1986) estimated that there are 600,000 to 650,000 Buddhists in France, with 150,000 French converts among them.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In 1999, sociologist Frédéric Lenoir estimated there are 10,000 converts and up to five million "sympathizers", although other researchers have questioned these numbers.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

[Taisen Deshimaru](/wiki/Taisen_Deshimaru "Taisen Deshimaru") was a Japanese [Zen Buddhist](/wiki/Zen_Buddhism "Zen Buddhism") who founded numerous [zendos](/wiki/Zendo "Zendo") in France. [Thich Nhat Hanh](/wiki/Thich_Nhat_Hanh "Thich Nhat Hanh"), a [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize")-nominated, [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam")-born Zen Buddhist, founded the [Unified Buddhist Church](/wiki/Unified_Buddhist_Church "Unified Buddhist Church") (Eglise Bouddhique Unifiée) in France in 1969. The [Plum Village Monastery](/wiki/Plum_Village_Monastery "Plum Village Monastery") in the [Dordogne](/wiki/Dordogne "Dordogne") in southern France was his residence and the headquarters of his international [sangha](/wiki/Plum_Village_Tradition "Plum Village Tradition").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Temple_des_1000_Boudhas_2.JPG/330px-Temple_des_1000_Boudhas_2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Temple_des_1000_Boudhas_2.JPG)

[Temple of One Thousand Buddhas](/wiki/Temple_of_One_Thousand_Buddhas "Temple of One Thousand Buddhas"), in [La Boulaye](/wiki/La_Boulaye "La Boulaye"), [Saône-et-Loire](/wiki/Sa%C3%B4ne-et-Loire "Saône-et-Loire"), [Burgundy](/wiki/Burgundy_(French_region) "Burgundy (French region)")

In 1968 Leo Boer and Wener van de Wetering founded a [Zen](/wiki/Zen "Zen") group, and through two books made Zen popular in the Netherlands.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) The guidance of the group was taken over by Erik Bruijn,[[20]](#cite_note-20) who is still in charge of a flourishing community. The largest Zen group now is the Kanzeon Sangha, led by Nico Tydeman under the supervision of the American Zen master [Dennis Genpo Merzel](/wiki/Dennis_Genpo_Merzel "Dennis Genpo Merzel"), Roshi, a former student of Maezumi Roshi in Los Angeles. This group has a relatively large centre where a teacher and some students live permanently. Many other groups are also represented in the Netherlands, like the Order of Buddhist Contemplatives in Apeldoorn, the [Thich Nhat Hanh](/wiki/Thich_Nhat_Hanh "Thich Nhat Hanh") Order of Interbeing and the International Zen Institute Noorderpoort[[21]](#cite_note-21) monastery/retreat centre in Drenthe, led by Jiun Hogen Roshi.

Perhaps the most widely visible Buddhist leader in the world is [Tenzin Gyatso](/wiki/Tenzin_Gyatso "Tenzin Gyatso"), the current [Dalai Lama](/wiki/Dalai_Lama "Dalai Lama"), who first visited the United States in 1979. As the exiled political leader of [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet"), he has become a popular cause célèbre. His early life was depicted in Hollywood films such as *[Kundun](/wiki/Kundun "Kundun")* and *[Seven Years in Tibet](/wiki/Seven_Years_in_Tibet_(1997_film) "Seven Years in Tibet (1997 film)")*. He has attracted celebrity religious followers such as [Richard Gere](/wiki/Richard_Gere "Richard Gere") and [Adam Yauch](/wiki/Adam_Yauch "Adam Yauch"). The first Western-born Tibetan Buddhist monk was [Robert A. F. Thurman](/wiki/Robert_A._F._Thurman "Robert A. F. Thurman"), now an academic supporter of the Dalai Lama. The Dalai Lama maintains a North American headquarters at [Namgyal Monastery](/wiki/Namgyal_Monastery "Namgyal Monastery") in [Ithaca, New York](/wiki/Ithaca,_New_York "Ithaca, New York").

Lewis M. Hopfe in his "Religions of the World" suggested that "Buddhism is perhaps on the verge of another great missionary outreach" (1987:170).

### Christian missions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missionary&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Christian missions")]

Main articles: [Christian mission](/wiki/Christian_mission "Christian mission") and [List of Christian missionaries](/wiki/List_of_Christian_missionaries "List of Christian missionaries")

See also: [Jesuit reduction](/wiki/Jesuit_reduction "Jesuit reduction") and [Catholic missions](/wiki/Catholic_missions "Catholic missions")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Finska_Missionss%C3%A4llskapet_interior_02.JPG/250px-Finska_Missionss%C3%A4llskapet_interior_02.JPG)](/wiki/File:Finska_Missionss%C3%A4llskapet_interior_02.JPG)

*Lähetyskirkko*, a Christian mission church in [Ullanlinna](/wiki/Ullanlinna "Ullanlinna"), [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland")

A [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") missionary can be defined as "one who is to witness across cultures".[[2]](#cite_note-Thomas_Hale_2003-2) The [Lausanne Congress](/wiki/First_International_Congress_on_World_Evangelization "First International Congress on World Evangelization") of 1974, defined the term, related to Christian mission as, "to form a viable indigenous church-planting movement". Missionaries can be found in many countries around the world.

In [the Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible"), [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") is recorded as instructing the apostles to make [disciples](/wiki/Disciple_(Christianity) "Disciple (Christianity)") of all nations ([Matthew 28:19–20](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Matthew%2028:19–20&version=nrsv), [Mark 16:15–18](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Mark%2016:15–18&version=nrsv)). This verse is referred to by Christian missionaries as the [Great Commission](/wiki/Great_Commission "Great Commission") and inspires missionary work.

#### Historic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missionary&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Historic")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Rugendas_-_Aldea_des_Tapuyos.jpg/250px-Rugendas_-_Aldea_des_Tapuyos.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rugendas_-_Aldea_des_Tapuyos.jpg)

Village of Christianized Tapuyos Indians, [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") c. 1820 CE

The Christian Church expanded throughout the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") already in New Testament times and is said by tradition to have reached even further, to Persia ([Church of the East](/wiki/Church_of_the_East "Church of the East")) and to India ([Saint Thomas Christians](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Christians "Saint Thomas Christians")). During the Middle Ages, the Christian [monasteries](/wiki/Monasteries "Monasteries") and missionaries such as [Saint Patrick](/wiki/Saint_Patrick "Saint Patrick") (5th century) and [Adalbert of Prague](/wiki/Adalbert_of_Prague "Adalbert of Prague") (c. 956–997) propagated Christian teachings beyond the European boundaries of the old Roman Empire. In 596, Pope [Gregory the Great](/wiki/Gregory_the_Great "Gregory the Great") (in office 590–604) sent the [Gregorian Mission](/wiki/Gregorian_Mission "Gregorian Mission") (including [Augustine of Canterbury](/wiki/Augustine_of_Canterbury "Augustine of Canterbury")) into England. In their turn, Christians from Ireland (the [Hiberno-Scottish mission](/wiki/Hiberno-Scottish_mission "Hiberno-Scottish mission")) and from Britain ([Saint Boniface](/wiki/Saint_Boniface "Saint Boniface") (c. 675–754) and the [Anglo-Saxon mission](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_mission "Anglo-Saxon mission"), for example) became prominent in converting the inhabitants of central Europe.

During the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery"), the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") established a number of [missions](/wiki/Mission_(Christian) "Mission (Christian)") in the Americas and in other Western colonies through the [Augustinians](/wiki/Augustinians "Augustinians"), [Franciscans](/wiki/Franciscans "Franciscans"), and [Dominicans](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order") to spread Christianity in the New World and[[22]](#cite_note-22) to convert the [Native Americans](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") and other indigenous people. About the same time, missionaries such as [Francis Xavier](/wiki/Francis_Xavier "Francis Xavier") (1506–1552), as well as other [Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits"), Augustinians, Franciscans, and Dominicans, reached Asia and the [Far East](/wiki/Far_East "Far East"), and the Portuguese sent missions into Africa. Emblematic in many respects is [Matteo Ricci](/wiki/Matteo_Ricci "Matteo Ricci")'s [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") mission to China from 1582, which is often characterized as peaceful and non-violent. These missionary movements should be distinguished from others, such as the [Baltic Crusades](/wiki/Baltic_Crusades "Baltic Crusades") of the 12th and 13th centuries, which were often considered compromised in their motivation by designs of military conquest.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/The_Reception_of_the_Rev._J._Williams%2C_at_Tanna%2C_in_the_South_Seas%2C_the_Day_Before_He_Was_Massacred%2C_1841_%28B-088-015%29.jpg/250px-The_Reception_of_the_Rev._J._Williams%2C_at_Tanna%2C_in_the_South_Seas%2C_the_Day_Before_He_Was_Massacred%2C_1841_%28B-088-015%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Reception_of_the_Rev._J._Williams,_at_Tanna,_in_the_South_Seas,_the_Day_Before_He_Was_Massacred,_1841_(B-088-015).jpg)

English missionary [John Williams](/wiki/John_Williams_(missionary) "John Williams (missionary)"), active in the [South Pacific](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania")

Much contemporary Catholic missionary work has undergone profound change since the [Second Vatican Council](/wiki/Second_Vatican_Council "Second Vatican Council") of 1962–1965, with an increased push for indigenization and [inculturation](/wiki/Inculturation "Inculturation"), along with [social justice](/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice") issues as a constitutive part of preaching [the Gospel](/wiki/The_gospel "The gospel").

As the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") normally organizes itself along territorial lines and had the human and material resources, religious orders, some even specializing in it, undertook most missionary work, especially in the era after the collapse of the Roman Empire in the West. Over time, the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") gradually established a normalized Church structure in the mission areas, often starting with special jurisdictions known as apostolic prefectures and [apostolic vicariates](/wiki/Apostolic_vicariates "Apostolic vicariates"). At a later stage of development these foundations are raised to regular diocesan status with a local bishop appointed. On a global front, these processes were often accelerated in the later 1960s, in part accompanying political decolonization. In some regions, however, they are still in course.

Just as the Bishop of Rome had jurisdiction also in territories later considered to be in the Eastern sphere, so the missionary efforts of the two 9th-century [saints Cyril and Methodius](/wiki/Saints_Cyril_and_Methodius "Saints Cyril and Methodius") were largely conducted in relation to the West rather than the East, though the field of activity was central Europe.

The [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), under the [Orthodox Church of Constantinople](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_of_Constantinople "Orthodox Church of Constantinople"), undertook vigorous missionary work under the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") and its successor, the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"). This had lasting effects and in some sense is at the origin of the present relations of [Constantinople](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_of_Constantinople "Orthodox Church of Constantinople") with some sixteen Orthodox national churches, including the [Romanian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Romanian_Orthodox_Church "Romanian Orthodox Church"), the [Georgian Orthodox and Apostolic Church](/wiki/Georgian_Orthodox_and_Apostolic_Church "Georgian Orthodox and Apostolic Church"), and the [Ukrainian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Ukrainian_Orthodox_Church_(Moscow_Patriarchy) "Ukrainian Orthodox Church (Moscow Patriarchy)") (both traditionally said to have been founded by the missionary Apostle Andrew), and the [Bulgarian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Bulgarian_Orthodox_Church "Bulgarian Orthodox Church") (said to have been founded by the missionary Apostle Paul). The Byzantines expanded their missionary work in Ukraine after the [mass baptism in Kiev](/wiki/Baptism_of_Rus%27 "Baptism of Rus'") in 988. The [Serbian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Serbian_Orthodox_Church "Serbian Orthodox Church") had its origins in the conversion by Byzantine missionaries of the Serb tribes when they arrived in the Balkans in the 7th century. Orthodox missionaries also worked successfully among the Estonians from the 10th to the 12th centuries, founding the [Estonian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Orthodoxy_in_Estonia "Orthodoxy in Estonia").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Martires_de_elicura.jpg/250px-Martires_de_elicura.jpg)](/wiki/File:Martires_de_elicura.jpg)

[Jesuits](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus") who were martyred by the [Araucanian](/wiki/Mapuche "Mapuche") Indians in Elicura in 1612 CE

Under the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") of the 19th century, missionaries such as [Nicholas Ilminsky](/wiki/Nikolay_Ilminsky "Nikolay Ilminsky") (1822–1891) moved into the subject lands and propagated Orthodoxy, including through [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus"), [Latvia](/wiki/Latvian_Orthodox_Church "Latvian Orthodox Church"), [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova"), [Finland](/wiki/Finnish_Orthodox_Church "Finnish Orthodox Church"), [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia"), [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"), and [China](/wiki/Chinese_Orthodox_Church "Chinese Orthodox Church"). The Russian [St. Nicholas of Japan](/wiki/Nicholas_of_Japan "Nicholas of Japan") (1836–1912) took Eastern Orthodoxy to [Japan](/wiki/Japanese_Orthodox_Church "Japanese Orthodox Church") in the 19th century. The [Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church") also sent missionaries to [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") beginning in the 18th century, including Saint [Herman of Alaska](/wiki/Herman_of_Alaska "Herman of Alaska") (died 1836), to minister to the [Natives](/wiki/Alaska_Natives "Alaska Natives"). The [Russian Orthodox Church Outside Russia](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church_Outside_Russia "Russian Orthodox Church Outside Russia") continued missionary work outside Russia after the 1917 [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution_of_1917 "Russian Revolution of 1917"), resulting in the establishment of many new dioceses in the [diaspora](/wiki/Diaspora "Diaspora"), from which numerous converts have been made in Eastern Europe, North America, and Oceania.

Early [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") missionaries included [John Eliot](/wiki/John_Eliot_(missionary) "John Eliot (missionary)") and contemporary ministers, including [John Cotton](/wiki/John_Cotton_(Puritan) "John Cotton (Puritan)") and Richard Bourne, who ministered to the [Algonquin](/wiki/Algonquin_people "Algonquin people") natives who lived in lands claimed by representatives of the [Massachusetts Bay Colony](/wiki/Massachusetts_Bay_Colony "Massachusetts Bay Colony") in the early 17th century. Quaker "publishers of truth" visited Boston and other mid-17th-century colonies but were not always well received.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The Danish government began the first organized Protestant mission work through its [College of Missions](/wiki/College_of_Missions "College of Missions"), established in 1714. This funded and directed [Lutheran](/wiki/Church_of_Denmark "Church of Denmark") missionaries such as [Bartholomaeus Ziegenbalg](/wiki/Bartholomaeus_Ziegenbalg "Bartholomaeus Ziegenbalg") in [Tranquebar](/wiki/Tranquebar "Tranquebar"), India, and [Hans Egede](/wiki/Hans_Egede "Hans Egede") in [Greenland](/wiki/Kangeq "Kangeq"). In 1732, while on a visit in 1732 to [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen") for the coronation of his cousin King [Christian VI](/wiki/Christian_VI "Christian VI"), the [Moravian Church](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church")'s patron Nicolas Ludwig, Count von [Zinzendorf](/wiki/Zinzendorf "Zinzendorf"), was very struck by its effects and particularly by two visiting [Inuit](/wiki/Kalaallit_people "Kalaallit people") children converted by [Hans Egede](/wiki/Hans_Egede "Hans Egede"). He also got to know a slave from the [Danish colony](/wiki/Danish_Virgin_Islands "Danish Virgin Islands") in the [West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies "West Indies"). When he returned to [Herrnhut](/wiki/Herrnhut "Herrnhut") in [Saxony](/wiki/Electorate_of_Saxony "Electorate of Saxony"), he inspired the inhabitants of the village – it had fewer than thirty houses then – to send out "messengers" to the slaves in the West Indies and to the [Moravian missions in Greenland](/wiki/Moravian_missions_in_Greenland "Moravian missions in Greenland"). Within thirty years, Moravian missionaries had become active on every continent, and this at a time when there were fewer than three hundred people in Herrnhut. They are famous for their selfless work, living as slaves among the slaves and together with [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), including the [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape") and [Cherokee](/wiki/Cherokee "Cherokee") Indian tribes. Today, the work in the former mission provinces of the worldwide Moravian Church is carried on by native workers. The fastest-growing area of the work is in [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania") in Eastern Africa. The Moravian work in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") inspired [William Carey](/wiki/William_Carey_(missionary) "William Carey (missionary)") and the founders of the British [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist") missions. As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Missionary&action=edit), seven of every ten Moravians live in a former mission field and belong to a race other than Caucasian.

Much [Anglican](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") mission work came about under the auspices of the [Society for the Propagation of the Gospel in Foreign Parts](/wiki/United_Society "United Society") (SPG, founded in 1701), the [Church Missionary Society](/wiki/Church_Missionary_Society "Church Missionary Society") (CMS, founded in 1799), and the [Intercontinental Church Society](/wiki/Intercontinental_Church_Society "Intercontinental Church Society") (formerly the Commonwealth and Continental Church Society, originating in 1823).

#### Modern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missionary&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Modern")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Canyon_of_the_Little_Christians.jpg/250px-Canyon_of_the_Little_Christians.jpg)](/wiki/File:Canyon_of_the_Little_Christians.jpg)

The first recorded baptism in [Alta California](/wiki/Alta_California "Alta California") at [La Christianita Canyon](/wiki/La_Christianita_Canyon "La Christianita Canyon")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/44/Mayerhoff.jpg/250px-Mayerhoff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mayerhoff.jpg)

A Christian missionary of the [Wisconsin Lutheran Synod](/wiki/Wisconsin_Evangelical_Lutheran_Synod "Wisconsin Evangelical Lutheran Synod") going to the [Apache](/wiki/Apache "Apache")

With a dramatic increase in efforts since the 20th century, and a strong push since the *Lausanne I: The International Congress on World Evangelization* in Switzerland in 1974,[[24]](#cite_note-24) modern evangelical groups have focused efforts on sending missionaries to every ethnic group in the world. While this effort has not been completed, increased attention has brought larger numbers of people distributing [Bibles](/wiki/Bible "Bible"), [Jesus videos](/wiki/Jesus_(1979_film) "Jesus (1979 film)"), and establishing [evangelical](/wiki/Evangelical "Evangelical") churches in more remote areas.

Internationally, the focus for many years in the later 20th century was on reaching every "people group" with [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") by 2000. Bill Bright's leadership with Campus Crusade, the Southern Baptist [International Mission Board](/wiki/International_Mission_Board "International Mission Board"), The Joshua Project, and others brought about the need to know who these "[unreached people groups](/wiki/Unreached_people_group "Unreached people group")" are and how those wanting to tell about the Christian God and share a [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") could reach them. The focus for these organizations transitioned from a "country focus" to a "people group focus". (From "What is a People Group?" by Dr. Orville Boyd Jenkins: A "people group" is an ethnolinguistic group with a common self-identity that is shared by the various members. There are two parts to that word: ethno and linguistic. Language is a primary and dominant identifying factor of a people group. But there are other factors that determine or are associated with ethnicity.)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/The_missionary_ship_%22Duff%22_arriving_%28ca._1797%29_at_Otaheite%2C_lithograph_by_Kronheim_%26_Co.jpg/250px-The_missionary_ship_%22Duff%22_arriving_%28ca._1797%29_at_Otaheite%2C_lithograph_by_Kronheim_%26_Co.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_missionary_ship_%22Duff%22_arriving_(ca._1797)_at_Otaheite,_lithograph_by_Kronheim_%26_Co.jpg)

The missionary ship *Duff* arriving at [Tahiti](/wiki/Tahiti "Tahiti"), c. 1797

What can be viewed as a success by those inside and outside the church from this focus is a higher level of cooperation and friendliness among churches and denominations. It is very common for those working on international fields to not only cooperate in efforts to share their [gospel message](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel"), but view the work of their groups in a similar light. Also, with the increased study and awareness of different people groups, western mission efforts have become far more sensitive to the cultural nuances of those they are going to and those they are working with in the effort.

Over the years, as indigenous churches have matured, the church of the [Global South](/wiki/Global_South "Global South") (Africa, Asia, and Latin America) has become the driving force in missions. Korean and African missionaries can now be found all over the world. These missionaries represent a major shift in church history where the nations they came from were not historically Christian. Another major shift in the form of modern missionary work takes shape in the conflation of spiritual with contemporary military metaphors and practices. Missionary work as [spiritual warfare](/wiki/Spiritual_warfare "Spiritual warfare") (Ephesians, Chapter 6) weapons of a spiritual sense, is the primary concept in a long-standing relationship between Christian missions and militarization. Though when the Church establishes a governance, usually this results in a formation of a national or regional military. (Romans, Chapter 13) Despite the seeming opposition between the submissive and morally upstanding associations with prayer and violence associated with militarism, these two spheres interact in a dialectical way. Yet they when properly implemented they are entangled to support one another in the upholding of a civilizations morality and the prosecution and punishment of criminals. In some cases a nations military may fail to operate according to Godly principles and is not supported by the Church or missionaries, in other cases the military is made up of the Church congregants. The results of spiritual conflict are then present in different ways as prayer can be strategically used, for or against a military.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Nigeria, and other countries have had large numbers of their Christian adherents go to other countries and start churches. These non-western missionaries often have unparalleled success; because, they need few western resources and comforts to sustain their livelihood while doing the work they have chosen among a new culture and people.

Main articles: [London Missionary Society](/wiki/London_Missionary_Society "London Missionary Society"), [Church Mission Society](/wiki/Church_Mission_Society "Church Mission Society"), [China Inland Mission](/wiki/China_Inland_Mission "China Inland Mission"), [Church's Ministry Among Jewish People](/wiki/Church%27s_Ministry_Among_Jewish_People "Church's Ministry Among Jewish People"), [Baptist Missionary Society](/wiki/Baptist_Missionary_Society "Baptist Missionary Society"), and [Christianity in China](/wiki/Christianity_in_China "Christianity in China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Preaching_from_a_Waggon_%28David_Livingstone%29_by_The_London_Missionary_Society.jpg/250px-Preaching_from_a_Waggon_%28David_Livingstone%29_by_The_London_Missionary_Society.jpg)](/wiki/File:Preaching_from_a_Waggon_(David_Livingstone)_by_The_London_Missionary_Society.jpg)

[David Livingstone](/wiki/David_Livingstone "David Livingstone") preaching from a wagon

One of the first large-scale missionary endeavors of the British colonial age was the [Baptist Missionary Society](/wiki/Baptist_Missionary_Society "Baptist Missionary Society"), founded in 1792 as the Particular Baptist Society for the Propagation of [the Gospel](/wiki/The_gospel "The gospel") Amongst the Heathen.

The [London Missionary Society](/wiki/London_Missionary_Society "London Missionary Society") was an [evangelical](/wiki/Evangelical "Evangelical") organisation, bringing together from its inception both [Anglicans](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") and [Nonconformists](/wiki/Nonconformist_(Protestantism) "Nonconformist (Protestantism)"); it was founded in England in 1795 with missions in Africa and the islands of the South Pacific. The [Colonial Missionary Society](/wiki/Colonial_Missionary_Society "Colonial Missionary Society") was created in 1836, and directed its efforts towards promoting [Congregationalist](/wiki/Congregationalist "Congregationalist") forms of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") among "British or other European settlers" rather than indigenous peoples.[[26]](#cite_note-26)
[[27]](#cite_note-27) Both of these merged in 1966, and the resultant organisation is now known as the [Council for World Mission](/wiki/Council_for_World_Mission "Council for World Mission").

The [Church Mission Society](/wiki/Church_Mission_Society "Church Mission Society"), first known as the Society for Missions to Africa and the East, was founded in 1799 by evangelical Anglicans centred around the [anti-slavery](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Abolitionism in the United Kingdom") activist [William Wilberforce](/wiki/William_Wilberforce "William Wilberforce"). It bent its efforts to the [Coptic Church](/wiki/Coptic_Church "Coptic Church"), the [Ethiopian Church](/wiki/Ethiopian_Church "Ethiopian Church"), and India, especially [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala"); it continues to this day. Many of the [network of churches they established](/wiki/Church_planting "Church planting") became the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion").

In 1809, the London Society for Promoting Christianity Amongst the Jews was founded, which pioneered mission amongst the Jewish people; it continues today as the [Church's Ministry Among Jewish People](/wiki/Church%27s_Ministry_Among_Jewish_People "Church's Ministry Among Jewish People"). In 1865, the [China Inland Mission](/wiki/China_Inland_Mission "China Inland Mission") was founded, going well beyond British controlled areas; it continues as the OMF, working throughout [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia").

##### Maryknoll

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missionary&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Maryknoll")]

Further information: [Maryknoll](/wiki/Maryknoll "Maryknoll")

In Montreal in 1910, [Father James Anthony Walsh](/wiki/James_Anthony_Walsh "James Anthony Walsh"), a priest from Boston, met [Father Thomas Frederick Price](/wiki/Thomas_Frederick_Price "Thomas Frederick Price"), from North Carolina. They agreed on the need to build a seminary for the training of young American men for the foreign Missions. Countering arguments that the Church needed workers here[*[ambiguous](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*], Fathers Walsh and Price insisted the Church would not flourish until it sent missioners overseas.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Independently, the men had written extensively about the concept, Father Price in his magazine *Truth*, and Father Walsh in the pages of *A Field Afar*, an early incarnation of *Maryknoll Magazine*.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Winning the approval of the American hierarchy, the two priests traveled to Rome in June 1911 to receive final approval from [Pope Pius X](/wiki/Pope_Pius_X "Pope Pius X") for the formation of the Catholic Foreign Mission Society of America, now better known as the Maryknoll Fathers and Brothers.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

#### Latter Day Saints

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missionary&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Latter Day Saints")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/LDS_name_tags.jpg/250px-LDS_name_tags.jpg)](/wiki/File:LDS_name_tags.jpg)

The iconic black name tags of [missionaries](/wiki/Missionary_(LDS_Church) "Missionary (LDS Church)") of [the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints")

[The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") (LDS Church) has an active [missionary program](/wiki/Missionary_(LDS_Church) "Missionary (LDS Church)"). Young men between the ages of eighteen and twenty-five are encouraged to prepare themselves to serve a two-year, self-funded, full-time proselytizing mission. Young women who desire to serve as missionaries can serve starting at the age of eighteen, for one and a half years. Retired couples also have the option of serving a mission. Missionaries typically spend two weeks in a [Missionary Training Center](/wiki/Missionary_Training_Center "Missionary Training Center") (or two to three months for those learning a new language) where they study the scriptures, learn new languages when applicable, prepare themselves to teach the [Gospel](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel") of [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus"), and learn more about the culture and the people they live among. As of December 2019, the LDS Church had over 67,000 full-time missionaries worldwide[[31]](#cite_note-31) and over 31,000 Service Missionaries.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Hindu missions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missionary&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Hindu missions")]

[Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") was introduced into [Java](/wiki/Java "Java") by travellers from India in ancient times. Several centuries ago, many Hindus left Java for [Bali](/wiki/Bali "Bali") rather than convert to [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"). Hinduism has survived in Bali ever since.[[33]](#cite_note-33) [Dang Hyang Nirartha](/wiki/Dang_Hyang_Nirartha "Dang Hyang Nirartha") was responsible for facilitating a refashioning of [Balinese Hinduism](/wiki/Balinese_Hinduism "Balinese Hinduism"). He was an important promoter of the idea of moksha in Indonesia. He founded the Shaivite priesthood that is now ubiquitous in Bali, and is now regarded as the ancestor of all Shaivite pandits.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

[Shantidas Adhikari](/wiki/Shantidas_Adhikari "Shantidas Adhikari") was a [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") preacher from [Sylhet](/wiki/Sylhet "Sylhet") who converted King [Pamheiba](/wiki/Pamheiba "Pamheiba") of [Manipur](/wiki/Manipur "Manipur") to Hinduism in 1717.[[35]](#cite_note-SLM-35)

Historically, Hinduism has only recently had a large influence in western countries such as the United Kingdom, New Zealand, and Canada. Since the 1960s, many westerners attracted by the world view presented in Asian religious systems have converted to Hinduism.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Many native-born Canadians of various ethnicities have converted during the last 50 years through the actions of the [Ramakrishna Mission](/wiki/Ramakrishna_Mission "Ramakrishna Mission"), [ISKCON](/wiki/ISKCON "ISKCON"), [Arya Samaj](/wiki/Arya_Samaj "Arya Samaj") and other missionary organizations as well as due to the visits and guidance of Indian gurus such as Guru Maharaj, [Sai Baba](/wiki/Sai_Baba_of_Shirdi "Sai Baba of Shirdi"), and [Rajneesh](/wiki/Rajneesh "Rajneesh"). The International Society for Krishna Consciousness has a presence in New Zealand, running temples in Auckland, Hamilton, Wellington and Christchurch.

[Paramahansa Yogananda](/wiki/Paramahansa_Yogananda "Paramahansa Yogananda"), an Indian [yogi](/wiki/Yoga "Yoga") and [guru](/wiki/Guru "Guru"), introduced many westerners to the teachings of meditation and [Kriya Yoga](/wiki/Kriya_Yoga_school "Kriya Yoga school") through his book, *[Autobiography of a Yogi](/wiki/Autobiography_of_a_Yogi "Autobiography of a Yogi")*.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

[Swami Vivekananda](/wiki/Swami_Vivekananda "Swami Vivekananda"), the founder of the Ramakrishna Mission is one of the greatest Hindu missionaries to the West.

#### Ananda Marga missions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missionary&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Ananda Marga missions")]

[Ānanda Mārga](/wiki/Ananda_Marga "Ananda Marga"), [organizationally](/wiki/Organization "Organization") known as Ānanda Mārga Pracaraka Samgha (AMPS), meaning the *[samgha](/wiki/Samgha "Samgha")* (organization) for the [propagation](/wiki/Wave_propagation "Wave propagation") of the *[marga](/wiki/Buddhist_Paths_to_liberation "Buddhist Paths to liberation")* (path) of *[ananda](/wiki/Ananda "Ananda")* (bliss), is a [social](/wiki/Social_issues "Social issues") and [spiritual](/wiki/Spirituality "Spirituality") movement[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-Chryssides-39) founded in [Jamalpur](/wiki/Jamalpur,_Munger "Jamalpur, Munger"), [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), in 1955 by [Prabhat Ranjan Sarkar](/wiki/Prabhat_Ranjan_Sarkar "Prabhat Ranjan Sarkar") (1921–1990), also known by his spiritual [name](/wiki/Name "Name"),[[40]](#cite_note-40) Shrii Shrii Ánandamúrti.[[41]](#cite_note-41) Ananda Marga counts hundreds of [missions](/wiki/Ananda_Marga#Organisation_and_Activities "Ananda Marga") around the world through which its members carry out various forms of selfless service on Relief. (The social welfare and development organization under AMPS is Ananda Marga Universal Relief Team, or AMURT.)[[42]](#cite_note-42) Education and women's welfare The service activities of this section founded in 1963 are focused on:[[43]](#cite_note-43)

* Education: creating and managing primary, post-primary, and higher [schools](/wiki/School "School"), research institutes
* Relief: creating and managing children's and students' homes for destitute children and for poor students, cheap hostels, retiring homes, academies of light for deaf dumb and crippled, invalid homes, refugee rehabilitation
* Tribal: tribal welfare units, medical camps
* Women's welfare: women welfare units, women's homes, nursing homes

### Islamic missions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missionary&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Islamic missions")]

Main article: [Islamic missionary activity](/wiki/Islamic_missionary_activity "Islamic missionary activity")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/7/76/Mission_Dawah.gif)](/wiki/File:Mission_Dawah.gif)

Mission Dawah is one of the largest contemporary Islamic missionary organizations.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Lingshan_Islamic_Cemetery_-_two_worthies_-_DSCF8405.JPG/500px-Lingshan_Islamic_Cemetery_-_two_worthies_-_DSCF8405.JPG)](/wiki/File:Lingshan_Islamic_Cemetery_-_two_worthies_-_DSCF8405.JPG)

The tombs of legendary Islamic missionaries in [China](/wiki/China "China"), [Sa-Ke-Zu and Wu-Ko-Shun](/wiki/Lingshan_Islamic_Cemetery#Holy_Tombs "Lingshan Islamic Cemetery") at Mount Lingshan, [Quanzhou](/wiki/Quanzhou "Quanzhou")

[Dawah](/wiki/Dawah "Dawah") means to "invite" (in Arabic, literally "calling") to [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), which is the second largest religion with 2.0 billion members.[[44]](#cite_note-44) From the 7th century, it spread rapidly from the [Arabian Peninsula](/wiki/Arabian_Peninsula "Arabian Peninsula") to the rest of the world through the initial [Muslim conquests](/wiki/Early_Muslim_conquests "Early Muslim conquests") and subsequently with traders and explorers after the death of [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad").

Initially, the spread of Islam came through the Dawah efforts of Muhammad and his followers. After his death in 632 CE, much of the expansion of the empire came through conquest such as that of North Africa and later Iberia ([Al-Andalus](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus")). The [Islamic conquest of Persia](/wiki/Islamic_conquest_of_Persia "Islamic conquest of Persia") put an end to the [Sassanid Empire](/wiki/Sassanid_Empire "Sassanid Empire") and spread the reach of Islam to as far east as [Khorasan](/wiki/Greater_Khorasan "Greater Khorasan"), which would later become the cradle of Islamic civilization during the [Islamic Golden Age](/wiki/Islamic_Golden_Age "Islamic Golden Age") (622–1258 CE) and a stepping-stone towards the introduction of Islam to the [Turkic tribes](/wiki/Turkic_tribes "Turkic tribes") living in and bordering the area.

The missionary movement peaked during the [Islamic Golden Age](/wiki/Islamic_Golden_Age "Islamic Golden Age"), with the expansion of foreign trade routes, primarily into the [Indo-Pacific](/wiki/Indo-Pacific "Indo-Pacific") and as far south as the isle of [Zanzibar](/wiki/Zanzibar "Zanzibar") as well as the Southeastern shores of Africa.

With the coming of the [Sufism](/wiki/Sufism "Sufism") tradition, Islamic missionary activities increased. Later, the [Seljuk Turks](/wiki/Seljuk_Turks "Seljuk Turks")' conquest of [Anatolia](/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia") made it easier for missionaries to go lands that formerly belonged to the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"). In the earlier stages of the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), a [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_peoples "Turkic peoples") form of [Shamanism](/wiki/Shamanism "Shamanism") was still widely practiced in Anatolia, but soon lost ground to [Sufism](/wiki/Sufism "Sufism").

During the [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") presence in the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans"), missionary movements were taken up by people from aristocratic families hailing from the region, who had been educated in [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") or other major city within the Empire such as the famed *[madrassahs](/wiki/Madrassah "Madrassah")* and *[kulliyes](/wiki/Kulliye "Kulliye")*. Primarily, individuals were sent back to the place of their origin and were appointed important positions in the local governing body. This approach often resulted in the building of mosques and local *kulliyes* for future generations to benefit from, as well as spreading the teachings of Islam.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/World_Islamic_Mission_1.jpg/250px-World_Islamic_Mission_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:World_Islamic_Mission_1.jpg)

The [World Islamic Mission](/wiki/World_Islamic_Mission "World Islamic Mission")'s mosque in [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo"), Norway

The spread of Islam towards [Central](/wiki/Central_Africa "Central Africa") and West Africa had until the early 19th century been consistent but slow. Previously, the only connection was through Trans-Saharan trade routes. The [Mali Empire](/wiki/Mali_Empire "Mali Empire"), consisting predominantly of African and Berber tribes, stands as a strong example of the early Islamic conversion of the Sub-Saharan region. The gateways prominently expanded to include the aforementioned trade routes through the Eastern shores of the African continent. With the [European colonization of Africa](/wiki/Colonization_of_Africa "Colonization of Africa"), missionaries were almost in competition with the European Christian missionaries operating in the colonies.

There is evidence of Arab Muslim traders entering Indonesia as early as the 8th century.[[45]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-45) Indonesia's early people were animists, Hindus, and Buddhists.[[46]](#cite_note-46) However it was not until the end of the 13th century that the process of [Islamization](/wiki/Islamization "Islamization") began to spread throughout the areas local communities and port towns.[[45]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-45) The spread, although at first introduced through Arab Muslim traders, continued to saturate through the Indonesian people as local rulers and royalty began to adopt the religion subsequently leading their subjects to mirror their conversion.

Recently, Muslim groups have engaged in missionary work in Malawi. Much of this is performed by the [African Muslim Agency](/wiki/African_Muslim_Agency "African Muslim Agency") based in [Angola](/wiki/Angola "Angola"). The [Kuwait](/wiki/Kuwait "Kuwait")-sponsored AMA has translated the [Qur'an](/wiki/Qur%27an "Qur'an") into [Chichewa](/wiki/Chichewa_language "Chichewa language") (Cinyanja),[[47]](#cite_note-47) one of the official languages of Malawi, and has engaged in other missionary work in the country. All of the major cities in the country have mosques and there are several Islamic schools.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

Several [South African](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), [Kuwaiti](/wiki/Kuwait "Kuwait"), and other Muslim agencies are active in Mozambique, with one important one being the African Muslim Agency. The spread of Islam into West Africa, beginning with ancient [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana") in the 9th century, was mainly the result of the commercial activities of North African Muslims. The empires of both [Mali](/wiki/Mali_Empire "Mali Empire") and [Songhai](/wiki/Songhai_Empire "Songhai Empire") that followed ancient Ghana in the Western Sudan adopted the religion. Islam made its entry into the northern territories of modern Ghana around the 15th century. [Mande](/wiki/Mande_languages "Mande languages") speakers (who in Ghana are known as [Wangara](/wiki/Soninke_Wangara "Soninke Wangara")) traders and clerics carried the religion into the area. The northeastern sector of the country was also influenced by an influx of [Hausa](/wiki/Hausa_people "Hausa people") Muslim traders from the 16th century onwards

Islamic influence first occurred in India in the early 7th century with the advent of Arab traders. Trade relations have existed between Arabia and the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") from ancient times. Even in the [pre-Islamic era](/wiki/Pre-Islamic_Arabia "Pre-Islamic Arabia"), Arab traders used to visit the [Malabar region](/wiki/Malabar_region "Malabar region"), which linked them with the ports of [Southeast Asia](/wiki/South_East_Asia "South East Asia"). According to Historians Elliot and Dowson in their book *[The History of India as told by its own Historians](/wiki/The_History_of_India_as_told_by_its_own_Historians "The History of India as told by its own Historians")*, the first ship bearing Muslim travelers was seen on the Indian coast as early as 630 CE H. G. Rawlinson, in his book: *Ancient and Medieval History of India* claims the first [Arab Muslims](/wiki/Arab_Muslims "Arab Muslims") settled on the Indian coast in the last part of the 7th century.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Shaykh Zainuddin Makhdum's "Tuhfat al-Mujahidin" also is a reliable work.[[50]](#cite_note-50) This fact is corroborated, by J. Sturrock in his *South Kanara and Madras Districts Manuals*,[[51]](#cite_note-51) and also by Haridas Bhattacharya in *Cultural Heritage of India Vol. IV*.[[52]](#cite_note-52) It was with the advent of Islam that the Arabs became a prominent cultural force in the world. The Arab merchants and traders became the carriers of the new religion, and they propagated it wherever they went.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

Islam in Bulgaria can be traced back to the mid-ninth century when there were Islamic missionaries in Bulgaria, evidenced by a letter from Pope Nicholas to [Boris of Bulgaria](/wiki/Boris_of_Bulgaria "Boris of Bulgaria") calling for the extirpation of Saracens.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

Pioneer Muslim missionaries to the Kenyan interior were largely [Tanganyikan](/wiki/Tanzania_Mainland "Tanzania Mainland"), who coupled their missionary work with trade, along the centres began along the railway line such as [Kibwezi](/wiki/Kibwezi "Kibwezi"), [Makindu](/wiki/Makindu "Makindu"), and [Nairobi](/wiki/Nairobi "Nairobi").

Outstanding among them was Maalim Mtondo [Islam in Kenya](/wiki/Islam_in_Kenya "Islam in Kenya"), a Tanganyikan credited with being the first Muslim missionary to Nairobi. Reaching Nairobi at the close of the 19th century, he led a group of other Muslims, and enthusiastic missionaries from the coast to establish a "Swahili village" in present-day [Pumwani](/wiki/Pumwani "Pumwani"). A small mosque was built to serve as a starting point and he began preaching Islam in earnest. He soon attracted several [Kikuyus](/wiki/Kikuyu_people "Kikuyu people") and Wakambas, who became his disciples.[[55]](#cite_note-Quraishy:1987:182-55)

In 1380, [Karim ul' Makhdum](/wiki/Makhdum_Karim "Makhdum Karim") the first Arabian [Islamic missionary](/wiki/Dawah "Dawah") reached the [Sulu Archipelago](/wiki/Sulu_Archipelago "Sulu Archipelago") and [Jolo](/wiki/Jolo "Jolo") in the Philippines and established Islam in the country. In 1390, the [Minangkabau](/wiki/Minangkabau_people "Minangkabau people")'s Prince [Rajah Baguinda](/wiki/Rajah_Baguinda "Rajah Baguinda") and his followers preached Islam on the islands.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The [Sheik Karimal Makdum Mosque](/wiki/Sheik_Karimal_Makdum_Mosque "Sheik Karimal Makdum Mosque") was the first mosque established in the Philippines on [Simunul](/wiki/Simunul,_Tawi-Tawi "Simunul, Tawi-Tawi") in [Mindanao](/wiki/Mindanao "Mindanao") in the 14th century. Subsequent settlements by Arab missionaries traveling to [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") and [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") helped strengthen Islam in the Philippines and each settlement was governed by a [Datu](/wiki/Datu "Datu"), [Rajah](/wiki/Rajah "Rajah"), and a [Sultan](/wiki/Sultan "Sultan"). Islamic provinces founded in the Philippines included the [Sultanate of Maguindanao](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Maguindanao "Sultanate of Maguindanao"), [Sultanate of Sulu](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Sulu "Sultanate of Sulu"), and other parts of the southern Philippines.

Modern missionary work in the United States has increased greatly in the last one hundred years, with much of the recent demographic growth driven by conversion.[[57]](#cite_note-57) Up to one-third of American Muslims are [African Americans](/wiki/African_American "African American") who have converted to Islam during the last seventy years. [Conversion to Islam in prisons](/wiki/Conversion_to_Islam_in_prisons "Conversion to Islam in prisons"),[[58]](#cite_note-autogenerated9-58) and in large [urban areas](/wiki/Urban_areas "Urban areas")[[59]](#cite_note-59) has also contributed to Islam's growth over the years.

An estimated US$45 billion has been spent by the Saudi Arabian government financing mosques and Islamic schools in foreign countries. *[Ain al-Yaqeen](/wiki/Ain_al-Yaqeen "Ain al-Yaqeen")*, a Saudi newspaper, reported in 2002 that Saudi funds may have contributed to building as many as 1,500 mosques and 2,000 other Islamic centers.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

During the [Expedition of Al Raji](/wiki/Expedition_of_Al_Raji "Expedition of Al Raji") in 625,[[61]](#cite_note-Mubarakpuri,_The_Sealed_Nectar_p._187-61) the Islamic Prophet [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad") sent some men as missionaries to various different tribes. Some men came to Muhammad and requested that Muhammad send instructors to teach them Islam,[[61]](#cite_note-Mubarakpuri,_The_Sealed_Nectar_p._187-61) but the men were bribed by the two tribes of Khuzaymah who wanted revenge for the [assassination of Khalid bin Sufyan](/wiki/Expedition_of_Abdullah_Ibn_Unais "Expedition of Abdullah Ibn Unais") (Chief of the [Banu Lahyan](/wiki/Banu_Lahyan "Banu Lahyan") tribe) by Muhammad's followers[[62]](#cite_note-archive.org-62) 8 Muslim Missionaires were killed in this expedition.,[[61]](#cite_note-Mubarakpuri,_The_Sealed_Nectar_p._187-61) another version says 10 Muslims were killed[[63]](#cite_note-Dr._Mosab-63)

Then during the [Expedition of Bir Maona](/wiki/Expedition_of_Bir_Maona "Expedition of Bir Maona") in July 625[[64]](#cite_note-64) Muhammad sent some Missionaries at request of some men from the Banu Amir tribe,[[65]](#cite_note-Mubarakpuri,_The_Sealed_Nectar_p._188-65) but the Muslims were again killed as revenge for the [assassination of Khalid bin Sufyan](/wiki/Expedition_of_Abdullah_Ibn_Unais "Expedition of Abdullah Ibn Unais") by Muhammad's followers[[62]](#cite_note-archive.org-62) 70 Muslims were killed during this expedition[[65]](#cite_note-Mubarakpuri,_The_Sealed_Nectar_p._188-65)

During the [Expedition of Khalid ibn al-Walid (Banu Jadhimah)](/wiki/Expedition_of_Khalid_ibn_al-Walid_(Banu_Jadhimah) "Expedition of Khalid ibn al-Walid (Banu Jadhimah)") in January 630,[[66]](#cite_note-Abu_Khalil_226-66) Muhammad sent Khalid ibn Walid to invite the Banu Jadhimah tribe to Islam.[[67]](#cite_note-William_Muir_p._135-67) This is mentioned in the Sunni Hadith *[Sahih al-Bukhari](/wiki/Sahih_al-Bukhari "Sahih al-Bukhari")*, [5:59:628](https://web.archive.org/web/19700101010101/http://cmje.usc.edu/religious-texts/hadith/bukhari/059-sbt.php#005.059.628).[[68]](#cite_note-68)

#### Ahmadiyya Islam missions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missionary&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Ahmadiyya Islam missions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Jamia_Ahmadiyya_International.jpg/500px-Jamia_Ahmadiyya_International.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jamia_Ahmadiyya_International.jpg)

Jamia Ahmadiyya, Ghana

Missionaries belonging to the [Ahmadiyya](/wiki/Ahmadiyya "Ahmadiyya") thought of Islam often study at International Islamic seminaries and educational institutions, known as [Jamia Ahmadiyya](/wiki/Jamia_Ahmadiyya "Jamia Ahmadiyya"). Upon completion of their degrees, they are sent to various parts of the world including South America, Africa, North America, Europe, and the Far East as appointed by [Mirza Masroor Ahmad](/wiki/Mirza_Masroor_Ahmad "Mirza Masroor Ahmad"), present head and [Caliph](/wiki/Khalifatul_Masih "Khalifatul Masih") of the worldwide Ahmadiyya Muslim community. Jamia students may be appointed by the Caliph either as Missionaries of the community (often called Murrabi, Imam, or Mawlana) or as Qadis or Muftis of the Ahmadiyya Muslim community with a specialisation in matters of fiqh (Islamic Jurisprudence). Some Jamia alumni have also become Islamic historians such as the late Dost Muhammad Shahid, former Official Historian of the Ahmadiyya Muslim community, with a specialisation in tarikh (Islamic historiography). Missionaries stay with their careers as appointed by the Caliph for the rest of their lives, as per their commitment to the community.

### Jain missions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missionary&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Jain missions")]

According to [Jaina](/wiki/Jain "Jain") tradition, Mahavira's following had swelled to 14,000 monks and 36,000 nuns by the time of his death in 527 BCE[[69]](#cite_note-69)
For some two centuries the Jains remained a small community of monks and followers. However, in the 4th century BCE, they gained strength and spread from [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar") to [Orissa](/wiki/Orissa,_India "Orissa, India"), then so [South India](/wiki/South_India "South India") and westwards to [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat") and the [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab_region "Punjab region"), where [Jain communities](/wiki/Jain_communities "Jain communities") became firmly established, particularly among the mercantile classes.[[70]](#cite_note-70) The period of the [Mauryan dynasty](/wiki/Mauryan_dynasty "Mauryan dynasty") to the 12th century was the period of Jainism's greatest growth and influence. Thereafter, the Jainas in the South and Central regions lost ground in face of rising Hindu devotional movements. Jainism retreated to the West and Northwest, which have remained its stronghold to the present.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

Emperor [Samprati](/wiki/Samprati "Samprati") is regarded as the "Jain Ashoka" for his patronage and efforts to spreading Jainism in east India. Samprati, according to Jain historians, is considered more powerful and famous than Ashoka himself. Samprati built thousands of [Jain Temples](/wiki/Jain_Temples "Jain Temples") in India, many of which remain in use, such as the Jain temples at [Viramgam](/wiki/Viramgam "Viramgam") and [Palitana](/wiki/Palitana_temples "Palitana temples") (Gujarat), [Agar Malwa](/wiki/Agar_Malwa "Agar Malwa") ([Ujjain](/wiki/Ujjain "Ujjain")). Within three and a half years, he got one hundred and twenty-five thousand new temples built, thirty-six thousand repaired, twelve and a half million murtis, holy statues, consecrated and ninety-five thousand metal [murtis](/wiki/Murtis "Murtis") prepared. Samprati is said to have erected Jain temples throughout his empire. He founded Jain monasteries even in non-Aryan territory, and almost all ancient Jain temples or monuments of unknown origin are popularly attributed to him. It may be noted that all the Jain monuments of [Rajasthan](/wiki/Rajasthan "Rajasthan") and Gujarat, with unknown builders are also attributed to Emperor Samprati.

[Virachand Gandhi](/wiki/Virachand_Gandhi "Virachand Gandhi") (1864–1901) from [Mahuva](/wiki/Mahuva,_Bhavnagar "Mahuva, Bhavnagar") represented Jains at the first [Parliament of the World's Religions](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_World%27s_Religions "Parliament of the World's Religions") in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") in 1893 and won a silver medal. Gandhi was most likely the first Jain and the first Gujarati to travel to the United States, and his statue still stands at the Jain temple in Chicago. In his time he was a world-famous personality. Gandhi represented Jains in Chicago because the Great Jain Saint Param Pujya Acharya [Vijayanandsuri](/wiki/Vijayanandsuri "Vijayanandsuri"), also known as Acharya Atmaram, was invited to represent the Jain religion at the first [World Parliament of Religions](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_World%27s_Religions "Parliament of the World's Religions"). As Jain monks do not travel overseas, he recommended the bright young scholar Virchand Gandhi to be the emissary for the religion. Today there are 100,000 Jains in the United States.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

There are also tens of thousands of Jains located in the UK and Canada.

### Judaism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missionary&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Judaism")]

Historically, various Jewish sects and movements have been consistent in avoiding or even forbidding [proselytization](/wiki/Proselytization "Proselytization") (religion-to-religion conversion propaganda) to convert [gentiles](/wiki/Gentile "Gentile") ([non-Jews](/wiki/Non-Jews "Non-Jews")). They believe that gentiles do not need to convert to Judaism, due to [Abrahamic religions](/wiki/Abrahamic_religions "Abrahamic religions") being already under the [Seven Laws of Noah](/wiki/Seven_Laws_of_Noah "Seven Laws of Noah").[[73]](#cite_note-:2-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74)

[Chabad Lubavitch](/wiki/Chabad_Lubavitch "Chabad Lubavitch") has a sub-sect that has engaged in an effort to spread [Noahidism](/wiki/Noahidism "Noahidism") ([Seven Laws of Noah](/wiki/Seven_Laws_of_Noah "Seven Laws of Noah")) among non-Jews who follow none of the existing [Abrahamic religions](/wiki/Abrahamic_religions "Abrahamic religions").[[75]](#cite_note-75)

[Orthodox Judaism outreach](/wiki/Orthodox_Judaism_outreach "Orthodox Judaism outreach") (*kiruv*) encourages non-practicing [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews") to become more knowledgeable and observant of *[halakha](/wiki/Halakha "Halakha")* (Jewish law). Outreach is done worldwide, by organizations such as [Chabad Lubavitch](/wiki/Chabad_Lubavitch "Chabad Lubavitch"), [Aish HaTorah](/wiki/Aish_HaTorah "Aish HaTorah"), [Ohr Somayach](/wiki/Ohr_Somayach,_Jerusalem "Ohr Somayach, Jerusalem"), and [Partners In Torah](/wiki/Partners_In_Torah "Partners In Torah").[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)

Members of [Reform Judaism](/wiki/Reform_Judaism "Reform Judaism") began a program to convert to their brand of Judaism the non-Jewish spouses of its [intermarried](/wiki/Interfaith_marriage_in_Judaism "Interfaith marriage in Judaism") members and non-Jews who have an interest in Reform Judaism. Their rationale is that so many Jews were lost during [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust") that newcomers must be sought out and welcomed.[[78]](#cite_note-78) This approach has been rejected by both [Orthodox Judaism](/wiki/Orthodox_Judaism "Orthodox Judaism") and [Conservative Judaism](/wiki/Conservative_Judaism "Conservative Judaism")[[79]](#cite_note-79) as unrealistic and posing a danger on the entire [Jewish faith](/wiki/Jewish_faith "Jewish faith").[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81)

### Sikh missions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missionary&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Sikh missions")]

According to [Sikhs](/wiki/Sikh "Sikh"), when he was twenty-eight, [Guru Nanak](/wiki/Guru_Nanak "Guru Nanak") went as usual down to the river to bathe and [meditate](/wiki/Meditate "Meditate"). It was said that he was gone for three days. When he reappeared, it is said he was "filled with the spirit of God". His first words after his re-emergence were: "there is no Hindu, there is no Muslim". With this [secular](/wiki/Secular "Secular") principle he began his missionary work.[[82]](#cite_note-Shackle_2005-82) He made four distinct major journeys, in the four different directions, which are called *[Udasis](/wiki/Udasis "Udasis")*, spanning many thousands of kilometres, preaching the message of God.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

Currently there are [gurdwaras in over 50 countries](/wiki/List_of_gurdwaras "List of gurdwaras").[[84]](#cite_note-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86)

Of missionary organizations, the most famous is probably [The Sikh Missionary Society UK](/wiki/The_Sikh_Missionary_Society_UK "The Sikh Missionary Society UK"). The aim of the Sikh Missionary Society is the *Advancement of the Sikh faith in the U.K. and abroad*, engages in various activities:[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90)

* Produce and distribute books on the Sikh faith in English and Panjabi, and other languages to enlighten the younger generation of Sikhs as well as non-Sikhs.
* Advise and support young students in schools, colleges, and universities on Sikh issues and Sikh traditions.
* Arrange classes, lectures, seminars, conferences, Gurmat camps and the celebration of holy Sikh events, the basis of their achievement and interest in the field of the Sikh faith and the [Panjabi language](/wiki/Panjabi_language "Panjabi language").
* Make available all Sikh artifacts, posters, literature, music, educational videos, DVDs, and multimedia CD-ROMs.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

There have been several Sikh missionaries:

* [Bhai Gurdas](/wiki/Bhai_Gurdas "Bhai Gurdas") (1551–1636), [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_people "Punjabi people") [Sikh](/wiki/Sikh "Sikh") writer, historian, missionary, and religious figure; the original scribe of the [Guru Granth Sahib](/wiki/Guru_Granth_Sahib "Guru Granth Sahib") and a companion of four of the [Sikh Gurus](/wiki/Sikh_Gurus "Sikh Gurus")[[92]](#cite_note-s-92)
* [Giani Pritam Singh Dhillon](/wiki/Giani_Pritam_Singh_Dhillon "Giani Pritam Singh Dhillon"), [Indian freedom fighter](/wiki/Indian_independence_movement "Indian independence movement")
* [Bhai Amrik Singh](/wiki/Bhai_Amrik_Singh "Bhai Amrik Singh"), devoted much of his life to Sikh missionary activities; one of the Sikh community's most prominent leaders along with [Sant Jarnail Singh Bhindranwale](/wiki/Sant_Jarnail_Singh_Bhindranwale "Sant Jarnail Singh Bhindranwale")
* [Jathedar Sadhu Singh Bhaura](/wiki/Jathedar_Sadhu_Singh_Bhaura "Jathedar Sadhu Singh Bhaura") (1905–1984), Sikh missionary who rose to be the [Jathedar](/wiki/Jathedar "Jathedar") or high priest of Sri [Akal Takhat](/wiki/Akal_Takhat "Akal Takhat"), [Amritsar](/wiki/Amritsar "Amritsar")

Sikhs have emigrated to many countries of the world since [Indian independence](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") in 1947. Sikh communities exist in Britain, East Africa, Canada, the United States, Malaysia, and most European countries.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

### Tenrikyo missions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missionary&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Tenrikyo missions")]

[Tenrikyo](/wiki/Tenrikyo "Tenrikyo") conducts missionary work in approximately forty countries.[[94]](#cite_note-94) Its first missionary was a woman named Kokan who worked on the streets of Osaka.[[95]](#cite_note-95) In 2003, it operated approximately twenty thousand mission stations worldwide.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

## Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missionary&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Criticism")]

Contact of Christian missionaries with isolated tribes is asserted as a cause of the extinction of some tribes, such as extinction from infections and even simple diseases such as flu, to which many tribes have no immunity.[[97]](#cite_note-wiresen1-97)[[98]](#cite_note-wiresen2-98) Documented cases of European contact with isolated tribes show rapid health deterioration, such as the [Nambikwara tribe](/w/index.php?title=Nambikwara_tribe&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nambikwara tribe (page does not exist)").[[99]](#cite_note-LeaveAlone1-99)

Christian missionary work is criticized by some as a form of colonialism.[[100]](#cite_note-100) Some Christian missionary thinkers have recognized complicity between colonialism and missions with roots in 'colonial paternalism'.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

Aspects of Christian missionary activity have come under criticism, including concerns about a lack of respect for other cultures.[[102]](#cite_note-102) The potential destruction of social structure among the converts is also a concern. The [Huaorani people](/wiki/Huaorani_people "Huaorani people") of Amazonian Ecuador have had a well-documented [mixed relation](/wiki/Operation_Auca "Operation Auca") with [Evangelical Christian](/wiki/Evangelical_Christian "Evangelical Christian") missionaries and the contacts they brought to their communities, which some have argued led to the dissolving of unique Huaorani tribes and cultural practices.

## Impact of missions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Missionary&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Impact of missions")]

John D. Woodberry argued that missions are parts of the roots of international liberal democracy[[103]](#cite_note-:1-103) however a 2020 study by Elena Nikolova and Jakub Polansky replicated Woodberry's analysis using twenty-six alternative democracy measures and a more extensive time period over which the democracy measures are averaged, and did not come to the same conclusion.[[104]](#cite_note-104)

A 2017 study found that areas of colonial Mexico that had Mendicant missions have higher rates of literacy and educational attainment today than regions that did not have missions.[[105]](#cite_note-:0-105) Areas that had Jesuit missions are today indistinct from the areas that had no missions.[[105]](#cite_note-:0-105) The study also found that "the share of Catholics is higher in regions where Catholic missions of any kind were a historical present."[[105]](#cite_note-:0-105)

A 2016 study found that regions in Sub-Saharan Africa that Protestant missionaries brought printing presses to are today "associated with higher newspaper readership, trust, education, and political participation."[[106]](#cite_note-106)[[107]](#cite_note-107)

Missionaries have also made significant [contributions to linguistics](/wiki/Missionary_linguistics "Missionary linguistics") and the description and documentation of many languages. "Many languages today exist only in missionary records. More than anywhere else, our knowledge of the native languages in South America has been the product of missionary activity... Without missionary documentation the reclamation [of several languages] would have been completely impossible"[[108]](#cite_note-108) "A satisfactory history of linguistics cannot be written before the impressive contribution of missionaries is recognised."[[109]](#cite_note-109)
